question: What is Babylon in Revelation 14:8? Is it Rome, a past empire, a future rebuilt city, or a religious system? Why does the harlotry vocabulary (porneia, porne) require a prior covenant relationship with God — making Babylon a corrupted form of the true faith rather than mere paganism? What does the zanah/porneia word chain from the OT prophets (Ezek 16, 23, Hosea 2) reveal about Babylon's character? What does the mother-daughter structure (Rev 17:5, 'MOTHER OF HARLOTS') reveal about Babylon's reproductive capacity? How does the balal etymology (Gen 11:9, 'confusion') establish mixture/confusion as Babylon's essential character? Address the preterist reading (Babylon = 1st-century Rome) and the futurist reading (Babylon = literal rebuilt city) — show why the harlot-bride bracket structure (Rev 17:1 and 21:9 using the same deictic formula) requires a symbolic religious-system reading.
prior_research_summary: Ten prior 3AM studies (00-09) completed. The first angel's message (studies 04-09) establishes the positive gospel against which Babylon's fall is measured. OT/NT pattern studies (02-03) trace the three-part structure including 'expose false system.' Four additional prior studies directly relevant: rev-14-babylon-three-angels (comprehensive Babylon identity study with 0.763 harlot-bride bracket score), rev-17-great-harlot-beast (Rev 17 exposition, 11 verbal parallels), rev-18-babylon-fall-come-out (Rev 18 composite oracle from 5 OT sources), revs-06-babylon-bride-structural-mirror (harlot-bride antithetical mirror documented through 11 verbal parallels, VP001-VP030). External corpus (EGW, Uriah Smith, Bohr) unanimously identifies Babylon as apostate religion with a mother-daughter structure. Web research gathered five major scholarly positions (preterist/Rome, preterist/Jerusalem, idealist, dispensational, futurist/literal) with 12 specific research directives to verify.

required_verses.reason: The second angel's message — crux text declaring Babylon's fall
The vision of the great harlot — garments, cup, name, blood
Woman on scarlet beast — priestly garments minus blue (Beale's claim)
MYSTERY BABYLON — mother of harlots and abominations
Seven mountains (oros not bounos) — test mountain vs. hill distinction
The harlot's identity — 'great city' reigning over kings, many waters = peoples
Beast destroys harlot — tests Rome-rides-Rome problem
Bride-city vision — bracket counterpart to 17:1-3
Babylon's fall proclaimed; 'Come out of her, my people' — God's people within
Pharmakeia and comprehensive blood-guilt — sorcery and martyr blood
Tower of Babel — balal etymology, confusion of languages, theological irony
Jerusalem as unfaithful bride — zanah vocabulary for covenant people
Judgment on the harlot Jerusalem — template for Rev 17:16
Oholah and Oholibah — two sisters of harlotry, Samaria and Jerusalem
Judgment on both sisters — zanah vocabulary and covenant context
ki-zanoh tizneh — God commands Hosea to marry a harlot symbolizing Israel
Israel as unfaithful wife — harlotry vocabulary in covenant context
Jerusalem called a harlot — 'faithful city become a harlot'
Israel's harlotry — zanah vocabulary for covenant unfaithfulness
Come out of Babylon — golden cup, intoxication of nations, flee command
Babylon as golden cup in the Lord's hand — direct source for Rev 17:4
Tyre called a harlot — counter-example to covenant-requirement claim
Nineveh called a harlot — counter-example to covenant-requirement claim
High priest's garments — gold, blue, purple, scarlet comparison with Rev 17:4
High priest's forehead inscription 'Holy to the LORD' — contrast with Rev 17:5
Babylon's garments listed — compare with LXX of Exod 28
Mystery of iniquity already at work — MYSTERION connection to Rev 17:5
The 'great city' where the Lord was crucified — same phrase used for Babylon?
The bride ready — fine linen, clean and white — contrast with harlot
Angel introduction formula — word-for-word comparison with 21:9-10
Babylon remembered before God — the cup of the wine of fierce wrath
Sudden destruction of Babylon — parallels Jer 51:8
Heaven rejoices, millstone cast — parallels Jer 51:48, 63-64
Judgment of the great harlot — vindication of martyrs
Prophecy against Babylon and its idols — OT Babylon judgment template
Flee from Babylon — OT come-out call
Permanent desolation of Babylon — fulfillment question
Bel punished in Babylon — nations no longer flow to her
Millstone sinking — Jeremiah's symbolic act, Rev 18:21 parallel
Burden of Babylon — Day of the Lord judgment language
Virgin daughter of Babylon — sorceries and enchantments
God's covenant with Jerusalem — the marriage metaphor foundation
Whoredom and wine take away the heart — zanah-wine connection
Israel broke yoke and played the harlot — zanah vocabulary
Peter writes from 'Babylon' — Rome identification evidence
Remnant who keep commandments — the faithful counterpart to Babylon
The second beast — lamblike horns, possible 'daughter' connection
Third angel's warning — consequence of following Babylon
Saints who keep commandments and faith of Jesus — the alternative to Babylon
Jezebel teaching fornication in the church — harlotry from within
Nebuchadnezzar's Babylon — 'Is not this great Babylon that I have built?'
Nimrod built Babel — origins of Babylon
Prohibition against harlotry — 'lest the land fall to whoredom'
No temple prostitution — zanah forbidden in worship context
Lamb overcomes beast — the final resolution
Blood-guilt principle — from Abel to Zechariah, one continuous phenomenon
Angel-worship bracket — frames the harlot-bride parallel
Angel-worship bracket conclusion — completes the structural parallel
Prophecy against Babylon — emotional response of the exiles
'Babylon is fallen, is fallen' — OT source of the doubled 'is fallen'
Jeroboam's state-sponsored apostasy — archetype of institutional false worship

focus_areas.title: The zanah/porneia word chain: harlotry requires covenant
focus_areas.what: Trace the harlotry vocabulary (zanah/porneia) across OT prophets and Revelation to determine whether it consistently presupposes a prior covenant relationship with God
focus_areas.why: If harlotry language exclusively addresses covenant partners, then Babylon cannot be mere paganism or a secular political empire — it must be a corrupted form of the true faith
focus_areas.how: Read H2181-zanah.md and G4202-porneia.md from the word-studies library. Parse every OT prophetic use of zanah for a city/nation (Ezek 16, 23; Hos 1-3; Isa 1:21; Jer 2:20; Jer 3:1-3). Evaluate the two counter-examples: Tyre (Isa 23:15-17) and Nineveh (Nah 3:4) — did these cities have treaty/covenant relationships with God? Run greek_parser.py on Rev 14:8, 17:2, 17:4, 18:3, 18:9, 19:2 to map porneia usage in Revelation. Check WG-fornication for the word group overview.
focus_areas.narrative: This focus area is the backbone of the entire study. READ `$WORD_STUDIES/H2181-zanah.md` for the full semantic range of zanah (highly-fed, wanton, covenant-unfaithfulness). READ `$WORD_STUDIES/G4202-porneia.md` and `$WORD_STUDIES/G4204-porne.md` for the NT vocabulary. The research agent must parse EVERY prophetic use of zanah applied to a city or nation — not just the famous ones. In Ezekiel 16, zanah and derivatives occur 21+ times describing Jerusalem, always presupposing the marriage covenant of Exod 19-24. In Hosea 1:2, the doubled construction ki-zanoh tizneh emphasizes habitual infidelity. The critical test cases are Tyre (Isa 23:15-17) and Nineveh (Nah 3:4), which some scholars cite as counter-examples. Run hebrew_parser.py on Isa 23:16-17 and Nah 3:4 to check whether the zanah usage differs. The Agape Bible Study source argues both cities had entered covenant — verify this. If the covenant requirement holds universally, it eliminates the pure-Rome and pure-paganism readings of Babylon. Run cross_testament_parallels_v2.py --hybrid-nt on Rev 14:8 for OT background.

focus_areas.title: The harlot-bride bracket structure (Rev 17:1-3 / 21:9-10)
focus_areas.what: Verify the verbal parallelism between the harlot introduction (17:1-3) and the bride introduction (21:9-10) and determine its structural implications
focus_areas.why: If the same angel, same formula, and same transport mechanism introduce both visions, then the harlot and bride are structurally antithetical — and if the bride is a symbolic community (the church), the harlot must also be a symbolic community (apostate religion)
focus_areas.how: Run greek_parser.py on Rev 17:1-3 and Rev 21:9-10. Compare word-by-word in Greek. Check the apophero + en Pneumati formula (occurs ONLY at 17:3 and 21:10 in the NT). Verify the angel-worship bracket (19:10 / 22:8-9). Review revs-06-babylon-bride-structural-mirror findings for the 11 verbal parallels already documented.
focus_areas.narrative: Bauckham (Climax of Prophecy) identifies Rev 17:1-19:10 and 21:9-22:9 as deliberately parallel sections. The prior study revs-06 documented 11 verbal parallels (VP001-002, VP022-030) with >75% element-by-element correspondence. The research agent should run greek_parser.py --verse on both Rev 17:1-3 and Rev 21:9-10 to verify word-for-word parallelism. The key phrase 'en Pneumati' occurs at four structural markers in Revelation (1:10; 4:2; 17:3; 21:10), and the last two create the harlot-bride frame. The angel-introduction formula shares 7+ consecutive Greek words between 17:1 and 21:9. The wilderness (eremos) vs. mountain (oros mega) contrast echoes OT prophetic geography. Run cross_testament_parallels_v2.py --hybrid-nt on Rev 17:1 and Rev 21:9 to map intertextual connections. The structural implication is decisive: if the bride is not literal Jerusalem but the church (a symbolic community), then the harlot cannot be literal Rome but must be apostate religion (a symbolic community).

focus_areas.title: The mother-daughter structure: METER TON PORNON
focus_areas.what: Determine whether 'mother of harlots' (meter ton pornon) in Rev 17:5 indicates the harlot generates other harlots or is merely a superlative title
focus_areas.why: If the title is relational/productive (the source generates daughters), Babylon is an archetype producing multiple historical manifestations — not reducible to any single entity. This eliminates the pure-Rome identification and supports the religious-system reading.
focus_areas.how: Run greek_parser.py on Rev 17:5 to parse meter ton pornon. Check how meter + genitive plural functions in Greek. Compare with OT 'mother' metaphors (Ezek 16:44-45). Check EGW/Smith/Bohr claims about the mother-daughter structure.
focus_areas.narrative: The phrase METER TON PORNON (μήτηρ τῶν πορνῶν) in Rev 17:5 is either relational (the source generating other harlots) or superlative (the greatest harlot of all). The research agent must parse Rev 17:5 thoroughly with greek_parser.py. In Greek, meter + genitive plural normally denotes origin or production (cf. 'mother of the living' in Gen 3:20 LXX). Compare with Ezek 16:44-45 where Jerusalem's 'mother' is established — run hebrew_parser.py on that passage. Storms/Alan Johnson argues that since Babylon is the 'mother,' no single historical manifestation can BE Babylon — they can only be her children. EGW (4SP 233.1) states 'Babylon the harlot is the mother of daughters who follow her example of corruption.' Uriah Smith (DAR 601.3, 658.2) argues the daughter designation proves Babylon extends beyond Rome. Verify whether the Greek grammar supports the productive reading. If confirmed, the mother-daughter structure means Babylon is a system generating offspring, not a single city.

focus_areas.title: The balal etymology: confusion and mixture as Babylon's essence
focus_areas.what: Verify the Hebrew wordplay in Gen 11:9 (balal → Babel) and determine whether 'confusion/mixture' establishes a theological characterization of Babylon
focus_areas.why: If balal's semantic range encompasses both confusion and mixture, Babylon's essential character is the mingling of truth and error — which fits a corrupt religious system better than a secular political empire
focus_areas.how: READ $WORD_STUDIES/H1101-balal.md and $WORD_STUDIES/H894-babel.md. Run hebrew_parser.py on Gen 11:9. Check LXX translation of balal (sygcheo?). Compare with Akkadian bab-ili (Gate of God). Evaluate the theological irony: humans call it 'gate of God,' God calls it 'confusion.'
focus_areas.narrative: READ `$WORD_STUDIES/H1101-balal.md` for the full semantic field of balal. The verb means 'to mix, confuse' — specifically 'to fill something with oil until it can absorb no more' (Abarim Publications), suggesting saturation. READ `$WORD_STUDIES/H894-babel.md` for the proper noun. Run hebrew_parser.py --verse on Gen 11:9 to confirm the morphology and the wordplay (ki-sham balal YHWH). The Akkadian bab-ili ('Gate of God') creates deliberate theological irony with the Hebrew balal ('confusion/mixture'). EGW (GC 383.1, HF 238.3) applies the 'confusion' meaning to denominational divisions. The research agent should also check whether the LXX translates balal with sygcheo (G4797, 'to pour together, confuse') and whether this Greek term appears in Revelation or related NT passages. The balal etymology establishes mixture as Babylon's essential character: what claims to be the 'gate of God' is actually confusion of truth and error.

focus_areas.title: Beale's priestly-garment claim: the harlot parodies the high priest
focus_areas.what: Verify whether the harlot's garments in Rev 17:4 and 18:16 match the LXX description of the high priest's vestments in Exodus 28, and whether the forehead inscription inverts the priest's 'Holy to the LORD'
focus_areas.why: If confirmed, this connection makes Babylon a religious parody of God's priesthood — not a secular political entity but a counterfeit religious system. The missing blue element (tekeleth/hyakinthos — the law-faithfulness color) would be especially significant.
focus_areas.how: Run greek_parser.py on Rev 17:4, Rev 18:16, and relevant LXX verses from Exod 28. Compare vocabulary: chrysion (gold), porphyra (purple), kokkinon (scarlet), byssinos (linen), lithos timios (precious stones). Check whether hyakinthos/blue appears in Exod 28 but is absent from Rev 17:4.
focus_areas.narrative: Beale (NIGTC Revelation) claims the combination of garment terms in Rev 17:4 is identical to the LXX description of the high priest's garments. The research agent must verify this by running greek_parser.py --verse on Rev 17:4 and Rev 18:16, then comparing with the LXX of Exod 28:4-6, 15-21, 36. The key vocabulary to compare: chrysion/chrysos (gold), porphyra/porphyrous (purple), kokkinon/kokkinos (scarlet), byssinos/byssos (fine linen), lithos timios (precious stones). The prior study rev-17 noted that the harlot's garments match priestly garments MINUS blue (hyakinthos/tekeleth) — the law-faithfulness element. Run hebrew_parser.py on Exod 28:36-38 for the forehead inscription 'Holy to the LORD' (qodesh la-YHWH) and compare with the harlot's forehead inscription 'MYSTERY BABYLON THE GREAT' (Rev 17:5). If Beale's claim holds, the harlot is a deliberate priestly parody — the strongest possible evidence for a religious-system reading.

focus_areas.title: The Rome-rides-Rome problem and the seven oros
focus_areas.what: Evaluate whether the preterist identification of both beast and harlot as Rome creates an internal contradiction, and whether oros in Rev 17:9 means mountain or hill
focus_areas.why: If the beast = Rome and the harlot = Rome, then Rev 17:3 (harlot rides beast) and Rev 17:16 (beast destroys harlot) are logically incoherent. The oros/bounos distinction could dismantle the seven-hills argument for Rome.
focus_areas.how: Run greek_parser.py on Rev 17:3, 17:9, 17:16. Verify Woods' claim that every other oros in Revelation means 'mountain' not 'hill.' Check whether bounos (G1015, 'hill') exists in Revelation. Parse the 'seven mountains on which the woman sits' — does 'sits' (kathestai) indicate location or dominion?
focus_areas.narrative: Andy Woods raises a significant objection to the Rome identification: if both the beast and the harlot represent Rome, then Rome rides upon Rome (17:3) and Rome destroys Rome (17:16-17). The research agent should run greek_parser.py --verse on Rev 17:3 (the woman sitting on the scarlet beast) and Rev 17:16 (the ten horns hate the harlot). Then examine Rev 17:9 where hepta ore (seven mountains) appears. Woods claims every other oros in Revelation means 'mountain' (6:14-16; 8:8; 14:1; 16:20; 21:10) not 'hill.' Verify this by running search_strongs.py --verses G3735 to find all oros occurrences in Revelation. If oros consistently means 'mountain' everywhere else in Revelation, the 'seven hills of Rome' reading requires oros to suddenly shift meaning in 17:9 alone — a hermeneutical problem. Also note that Rev 17:9b immediately adds 'there are also seven kings' — the mountains may represent kingdoms/powers, not geographic hills. The Agape Bible Study source notes the seven mountains identify the beast, not the woman — a critical distinction.

focus_areas.title: Jeremiah 50-51 / Revelation 17-18: the literary typology
focus_areas.what: Verify the 13 thematic parallels between Jeremiah's Babylon oracle and John's Revelation to establish that Revelation's Babylon is typological, not merely historical
focus_areas.why: John's deliberate reuse of Jeremiah's Babylon language establishes literary and theological continuity — Revelation's Babylon is the eschatological antitype of Jeremiah's historical Babylon, not the same literal city
focus_areas.how: Run cross_testament_parallels_v2.py on key verses from Rev 17-18 against Jer 50-51. Verify at least 6 of the 13 parallels documented by Precept Austin. Parse Jer 51:7 and Rev 17:4 in parallel (golden cup imagery).
focus_areas.narrative: Precept Austin documents 13 thematic connections between Jer 50-51 and Rev 17-18: golden cup (Jer 51:7 / Rev 17:3-4), intoxicating nations (51:7 / 17:2), many waters (51:13 / 17:1,15), divine flee command (51:6,45,50 / 18:4), sudden destruction (51:8 / 18:8-10), fire (51:30,58 / 17:16, 18:8), heaven rejoices (51:48 / 18:20), saint persecution (51:49 / 17:6, 18:24), millstone sinking (51:63-64 / 18:21), permanent desolation (50:39-40 / 18:2), deserved punishment (50:29 / 18:6), nations' outcry (50:46 / 18:9-19), city name (50:1 / 17:5,18). The research agent should run cross_testament_parallels_v2.py --hybrid-ot on Rev 17:4 and Rev 18:4 to map intertextual connections computationally. Parse Jer 51:7 and Rev 17:4 side by side using hebrew_parser.py and greek_parser.py respectively. The sheer density of Jeremianic allusion means John is constructing Revelation's Babylon as a typological development of Jeremiah's — the eschatological antitype that fulfills the type.

focus_areas.title: The wine of Babylon's thumos: intoxication vocabulary
focus_areas.what: Determine what the 'wine of the wrath (thumos) of her fornication' (Rev 14:8) means — divine wrath, passionate intoxication, or both — and trace the chemah→thumos lexical bridge
focus_areas.why: The word thumos is debated: does it refer to the passion/heat of Babylon's fornication, or God's wrath triggered by her fornication? The chemah (H2534) → thumos (G2372) chain connects OT wrath vocabulary directly to Revelation's Babylon passages
focus_areas.how: READ $WORD_STUDIES/G2372-thumos.md and TR-chemah-thymos.md. Run greek_parser.py on Rev 14:8, 14:10, 16:19, 18:3, 19:15. Parse the genitive chain: oinos tou thumou tes porneias — whose thumos? Compare with Rev 14:10 where thumos is clearly God's.
focus_areas.narrative: The phrase ek tou oinou tou thumou tes porneias autes in Rev 14:8 contains a complex genitive chain that scholars parse differently. READ `$WORD_STUDIES/G2372-thumos.md` for the semantic range (passion, fury, fierce anger). READ `$WORD_STUDIES/TR-chemah-thymos.md` for the OT-to-NT translation bridge — chemah in Jer 51:7 (cup of fury) becomes thumos in the LXX and then in Rev 14:8. Meyer argues thumos means divine wrath; Cambridge Bible argues for a blending of intoxicating passion and divine judgment. The research agent should run greek_parser.py --verse on Rev 14:8 and then compare with Rev 14:10 (where thumos + orge combine as clearly divine), Rev 16:19 (cup of the wine of the fierceness of God's wrath), and Rev 18:3 (wine of her fornication). Also READ `$WORD_STUDIES/G3709-orge.md` and `$WORD_STUDIES/H2534-chemah.md`. The polarity flip noted in prior study rev-14-babylon: Babylon's thumos (14:8) is answered by God's thumos + orge (14:10) — divine justice through exact correspondence.

focus_areas.title: MYSTERION: mystery of iniquity and the hidden nature of Babylon
focus_areas.what: Trace the mysterion connection between Rev 17:5 (MYSTERY on the harlot's forehead) and 2 Thess 2:7 (mystery of iniquity) and determine what this implies about Babylon's character
focus_areas.why: If Rev 17:5's MYSTERION links to Paul's 'mystery of lawlessness already at work,' Babylon is not a sudden end-time phenomenon but a concealed corruption operating throughout history
focus_areas.how: READ $WORD_STUDIES/G3466-mysterion.md and WG-mystery. Run greek_parser.py on Rev 17:5 and 2 Thess 2:3-7. Check whether MYSTERION in Rev 17:5 is part of the name or a separate label indicating symbolic interpretation.
focus_areas.narrative: The word MYSTERION (μυστήριον) on the harlot's forehead in Rev 17:5 is debated: is it part of the title ('Mystery Babylon the Great') or a meta-label ('this is a mystery: Babylon the Great')? READ `$WORD_STUDIES/G3466-mysterion.md` for the full range. The research agent should run greek_parser.py --verse on Rev 17:5 to check the grammar. Then compare with 2 Thess 2:3-7 where to mysterion tes anomias (the mystery of lawlessness) is described as 'already at work' in Paul's day. READ the WG-mystery word group entry. The prior study rev-17 connected MYSTERION + 'mystery of iniquity' (2 Thess 2:7) = concealed lawlessness already active. The apoleia chain noted in that study links the beast (Rev 17:8,11), the man of sin (2 Thess 2:3), and Judas (John 17:12) — all instances of betrayal from within. If MYSTERION signals symbolic meaning, the entire name becomes a theological description rather than a geographic identifier.

focus_areas.title: Preterist vs. futurist vs. historicist: testing each reading against the text
focus_areas.what: Evaluate the textual evidence for each major identification of Babylon (Rome, Jerusalem, literal rebuilt city, religious system) and determine which reading best accounts for ALL the textual data
focus_areas.why: The study must address criticism #13 ('Babylon is Rome/a past entity') by showing what each position gets right and where each fails against the full weight of the evidence
focus_areas.how: For each position, identify its strongest text and its weakest text. Test the Rome reading against the covenant-requirement argument. Test the Jerusalem reading against Rev 17:18 ('reigns over kings of the earth'). Test the futurist reading against the symbolic clues (MYSTERION, harlot-bride bracket). Test the historicist/religious-system reading against the seven-mountains passage.
focus_areas.narrative: This focus area is the synthesis of all other focus areas. The research agent should evaluate each major scholarly position: (1) PRETERIST/ROME (Aune, Charles, Swete, Caird): strongest evidence is 1 Pet 5:13, seven hills, Dea Roma coin; weakest point is the Rome-rides-Rome problem and the covenant-requirement for harlot language; (2) PRETERIST/JERUSALEM (Ford, Chilton, Gentry): strongest evidence is Rev 11:8 ('great city' = crucifixion site), harlot imagery mirrors OT accusations of Israel; weakest point is 'unprecedented' to call Jerusalem 'Babylon' and Rev 17:18 ('reigns over kings of earth'); (3) FUTURIST/LITERAL (Woods, Thomas): strongest evidence is unfulfilled OT Babylon prophecies (Jer 50:39-40); weakest point is MYSTERION label suggesting symbolic reading and the harlot-bride bracket; (4) IDEALIST (Beale, Hendriksen): strongest evidence is trans-historical principle, mother-daughter structure; weakest point is Rev 1:1 'things which must shortly come to pass'; (5) HISTORICIST/RELIGIOUS SYSTEM: strongest evidence is the cumulative convergence of harlotry vocabulary requiring covenant, priestly-garment parody, mother-daughter productive structure, harlot-bride bracket, and Babylon-Jerusalem antithesis. The research agent should retrieve full text of 1 Pet 5:13 and Rev 11:8 for direct comparison.

focus_areas.title: The abomination vocabulary: bdelygma, towebah, shiqquwts
focus_areas.what: Map the OT abomination vocabulary (towebah, shiqquwts) through the LXX bridge to bdelygma in Rev 17:4-5 to determine what specific practices constitute Babylon's 'abominations'
focus_areas.why: The 'abominations' in Babylon's golden cup have a specific OT referent — if towebah/shiqquwts consistently denote idolatry, false worship, and law-rejection, then Babylon's wine contains religious corruption, not merely political or economic sins
focus_areas.how: READ $WORD_STUDIES/G946-bdelygma.md, $WORD_STUDIES/H8441-towebah.md, $WORD_STUDIES/H8251-shiqquwts.md. Read TR-shiqquwts-bdelygma.md for the translation relationship. Run greek_parser.py on Rev 17:4-5.
focus_areas.narrative: READ `$WORD_STUDIES/G946-bdelygma.md` for the NT usage — bdelygma occurs 6 times, including the 'abomination of desolation' (Matt 24:15 / Dan 9:27). READ `$WORD_STUDIES/H8441-towebah.md` — the OT term covers idolatry (Deut 7:25; 27:15; 32:16), sorcery/necromancy (Deut 18:10-11), unjust weights (Deut 25:13-16; Prov 11:1), and sexual sin (Lev 18:22). READ `$WORD_STUDIES/H8251-shiqquwts.md` for the specific 'detestable idol' meaning. READ `$WORD_STUDIES/TR-shiqquwts-bdelygma.md` for the translation relationship. The research agent should parse Rev 17:4 (golden cup full of bdelygmata kai ta akatharta tes porneias autes) and check how the genitive chain works. The Nave's entry for ABOMINATION lists categories including idolatry, sorcery, false worship, and profanation of the sacred — verify whether these categories match the specific contents of Babylon's cup in Revelation 17-18.

focus_areas.title: The 'great city' designation: Babylon vs. Jerusalem in Revelation
focus_areas.what: Determine whether 'the great city' (he polis he megale) refers to the same entity in Rev 11:8 (where the Lord was crucified) and Rev 17:18 (which reigns over the kings of the earth)
focus_areas.why: The preterist/Jerusalem camp argues that 'the great city' consistently refers to Jerusalem throughout Revelation; if the phrase shifts referent, the Jerusalem identification of Babylon weakens significantly
focus_areas.how: Run greek_parser.py on Rev 11:8, Rev 14:8, Rev 16:19, Rev 17:18, Rev 18:10, 18:16, 18:18, 18:19, 18:21. Map every occurrence of he polis he megale in Revelation. Check whether contextual markers distinguish the referent.
focus_areas.narrative: The phrase he polis he megale ('the great city') appears multiple times in Revelation with potentially different referents. In Rev 11:8, it is explicitly identified as 'where also their Lord was crucified' — clearly Jerusalem. In Rev 17:18, the 'great city' 'reigns over the kings of the earth' — a description that fits Rome or a trans-historical power but not Jerusalem geographically. The research agent should parse every occurrence of this exact phrase in Revelation using search_strongs.py to find all co-occurrences of G4172 (polis) and G3173 (megas). Rev 16:19 splits 'the great city' into three parts, then mentions 'Babylon the great' separately — are they the same entity? The distinction between 'the great city' in 11:8 and 'the great city' in 17:18 may indicate that John uses the phrase for different entities, or that Jerusalem has been subsumed into the Babylon archetype. Run cross_testament_parallels_v2.py on Rev 11:8 and Rev 17:18.

required_word_studies.reason: Core harlotry verb — covenant-unfaithfulness in OT prophets
Harlotry/fornication — the NT equivalent of zanah in Revelation's Babylon passages
Harlot/prostitute — the noun identifying the woman in Rev 17
Verb 'to commit fornication' — used for nations' interaction with Babylon
Fornicator — male counterpart, kings who commit fornication with Babylon
The Greek proper noun — all 12 NT occurrences
The Hebrew proper noun — OT usage and etymological connection to balal
The verb 'to confuse/mix' — etymology of Babel/Babylon
Wrath/passion — the debated word in Rev 14:8's genitive chain
Wrath — distinguished from thumos; used in Rev 14:10 for God's wrath
Abomination — contents of Babylon's golden cup
OT abomination — source vocabulary for bdelygma through LXX
Mystery — on the harlot's forehead, linked to 2 Thess 2:7
To fall — 'Babylon is fallen, is fallen' (Rev 14:8) — prophetic aorist
Heat/fury — OT source for thumos through LXX translation bridge

external_corpus_leads.claim: Babylon's wine = specific false doctrines including false sabbath and immortal soul
external_corpus_leads.directive: Verify whether the text itself enumerates specific doctrines or provides categories (bdelygmata, porneia, pharmakeia). The text gives categories, not a doctrinal list.

external_corpus_leads.claim: Babylon's fall must apply to a religious body that was once pure and has become corrupt — the doubled 'is fallen' requires a transition from purity
external_corpus_leads.directive: Verify whether the prophetic aorist epesen epesen (Rev 14:8) implies a progressive fall from a prior state, or simply announces judgment. Compare Isa 21:9 (same doubled formula for historical Babylon).

external_corpus_leads.claim: The term 'confusion' appropriately applies to Protestant bodies divided into innumerable sects
external_corpus_leads.directive: Verify whether the balal etymology supports this application — does balal refer specifically to doctrinal mixture or to linguistic confusion or both?

external_corpus_leads.claim: The mother-daughter structure means Protestant churches clinging to Rome's doctrines are the 'daughters'
external_corpus_leads.directive: Verify whether the Greek grammar of METER TON PORNON supports a productive/relational reading (mother generates daughters) rather than a superlative ('greatest harlot').

external_corpus_leads.claim: Rome is Babylon but not to the exclusion of all other bodies — the mother-of-harlots title proves other religious organizations are included
external_corpus_leads.directive: Verify whether the text supports extending Babylon beyond a single entity to include 'daughter' organizations.

external_corpus_leads.claim: Babylon's fall is caused by rejecting offered light — clinging to false doctrines when corrective truth is available
external_corpus_leads.directive: Verify whether the text supports a causal connection between rejecting the first angel's message and Babylon's fall. Does Rev 14:8 follow 14:6-7 sequentially in narrative or logically?

external_corpus_leads.claim: The dragon/beast/false prophet trilogy (Rev 16:13) = the harlot/daughters/kings alliance
external_corpus_leads.directive: Verify whether the text supports equating these two triads. Does John use cross-referencing markers?

external_corpus_leads.claim: The harlot represents apostate religion allied with seven consecutive civil powers from Babylon to the papacy
external_corpus_leads.directive: Verify whether Rev 17's structure supports a sequential historical reading of the seven heads/mountains.

external_corpus_leads.claim: The beast with lamblike horns (Rev 13:11) = the daughters of the harlot
external_corpus_leads.directive: Verify whether the text identifies this connection or whether it is inferred. Check Rev 13:11-17 for harlot language.

external_corpus_leads.claim: Rev 17:1-19:10 and 21:9-22:9 are structurally parallel sections introduced by the same bowl angel
external_corpus_leads.directive: Verify by word-for-word Greek comparison of Rev 17:1-3 and Rev 21:9-10.

external_corpus_leads.claim: The harlot's garments in Rev 17:4 are identical to the LXX description of the high priest's garments in Exod 28
external_corpus_leads.directive: Compare the Greek vocabulary: chrysion, porphyra, kokkinon, byssinos, lithos timios between Rev 17:4/18:16 and LXX Exod 28.

external_corpus_leads.claim: If beast = Rome and harlot = Rome, then Rome rides Rome (17:3) and Rome destroys Rome (17:16) — logical contradiction
external_corpus_leads.directive: Verify from the text whether beast and harlot can be the same entity given their distinct interactions in Rev 17.

external_corpus_leads.claim: Every other oros in Revelation means mountain, not hill
external_corpus_leads.directive: Search all occurrences of G3735 (oros) in Revelation and verify whether any clearly mean 'hill.'

external_corpus_leads.claim: Harlot metaphor is used exclusively for covenant-breaking Israel with only two exceptions: Tyre and Nineveh — both cities that had entered covenant with God
external_corpus_leads.directive: Verify comprehensively: list every OT city/nation called a harlot (zanah) and determine covenant status.

external_corpus_leads.claim: Even a Rome-advocate acknowledges 'the harlot metaphor requires prior covenant relationship'
external_corpus_leads.directive: Test this claim against the full OT evidence, including the Tyre/Nineveh counter-examples.

external_corpus_leads.claim: Systematic biblical interpretation yields a historicist identification of Babylon as papal Rome
external_corpus_leads.directive: Evaluate Reynolds' textual arguments — does consistent biblical interpretation favor the historicist reading over alternatives?

external_corpus_leads.claim: Babylon cannot be confined to any one historical manifestation — the mother designation makes her the archetypal source
external_corpus_leads.directive: Verify whether the mother-daughter structure requires an archetypal rather than singular identification.

external_corpus_leads.claim: Babylon belongs not to one age — she has reared palaces on the Seine, and on the Thames
external_corpus_leads.directive: Test whether the biblical text supports trans-historical application of the Babylon archetype.

research_instructions:
## Critical Research Directives

### 1. Verify the Harlot-Bride Bracket in Greek
Compare Rev 17:1-3 with Rev 21:9-10 word-by-word in Greek. Count shared words. Confirm that apophero + en Pneumati occurs ONLY at these two verses in the NT. Document the wilderness (eremos) vs. mountain (oros mega) contrast.

### 2. Test Covenant-Requirement Comprehensively
List EVERY OT city/nation addressed with zanah vocabulary. For each, note whether it had covenant relationship with YHWH. For Tyre (Isa 23:15-17) and Nineveh (Nah 3:4), investigate whether treaty/covenant relationships existed. The web research notes that Agape Bible Study claims both had entered covenant — verify this.

### 3. Verify Beale's Priestly-Garment Claim Against LXX
Compare the EXACT Greek vocabulary in Rev 17:4 and Rev 18:16 with LXX Exod 28:4-6, 15-21, 36. Check for chrysion/chrysos, porphyra/porphyrous, kokkinon/kokkinos, byssinos/byssos, lithos timios. Note whether hyakinthos (blue) appears in Exod 28 but is ABSENT from Rev 17:4 — the missing color would be significant.

### 4. Map the Chemah→Thumos Translation Bridge
READ TR-chemah-thymos.md for the established bridge. Then verify that chemah in Jer 51:7 becomes thumos in Rev 14:8 through the LXX. Parse the complex genitive chain in Rev 14:8 carefully.

### 5. Evaluate the Prophetic Aorist in Rev 14:8
READ the grammar reference on prophetic aorist (tense-aorist-prophetic.md). Parse epesen epesen ('is fallen, is fallen') — is this a prophetic aorist (certainty of future event) or a statement of present reality? Compare with Isa 21:9 which uses the same doubled formula.

### 6. Series Context
This is Study 10, the first in Arc 2 (The Second Angel — Babylon Exposed). It builds on the first angel's gospel message established in studies 04-09. The positive gospel standard (fear God, give glory, worship the Creator) is the measure against which Babylon's corruption is assessed. Cross-reference the commandment inclusio (Rev 12:17 → 14:12) and the worship binary (proskyneo vocabulary) established in 3am-08.

### 7. Do NOT Duplicate Prior Studies
Four prior studies (rev-14-babylon-three-angels, rev-17-great-harlot-beast, rev-18-babylon-fall-come-out, revs-06-babylon-bride-structural-mirror) already cover much of this territory. This study should go DEEPER on the specific question of identity, testing the covenant-requirement argument, the priestly-garment claim, the mother-daughter grammar, and the five scholarly positions with fresh research rather than merely restating prior conclusions.
